title: Understand: sound waves
description: Check your understanding of sound waves in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about sound waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Sound waves need a matter medium to travel through.
All sound waves have the same amplitude and frequency.
Human ears can hear all sound waves directly.
Sound waves travel at different speeds in different mediums.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about sound waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Sound waves can travel through matter or through vacuum.
Different sound waves can have different amplitudes and frequencies.
Human ears cannot hear all sound waves directly.
Sound waves travel at the same speed in all mediums.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statements about sound waves.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Sound waves need a matter medium to travel through.
All sound waves have the same amplitude and frequency.
Human ears cannot hear all sound waves directly.
Sound waves travel at the same speed in all mediums.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two speakers are located in the same room filled with air. One plays a high-pitched sound and the other plays a low-pitched sound. Both sounds have the same loudness.

**Which wave properties are different for the two sounds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A sound wave's pitch is related to its frequency. The high-pitched sound has a higher frequency than the low-pitched sound.

All sound waves travel at the same speed *in the same medium*. Both speakers are playing in the same air. So, the sounds from both of them travel outward through the air at the same speed.

Since both sound waves travel at the same speed, a higher frequency corresponds to a shorter wavelength, and vice versa. As one increases the other decreases. So, the high-pitched sound has a shorter wavelength than the low-pitched sound.
A sound wave's loudness is related to its amplitude. We're told that both sounds have the same loudness. So, they have the same amplitude.
The wave properties that are different for the two sounds are wavelength and frequency.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two speakers are located in the same room filled with air. One plays a high-pitched sound and the other plays a low-pitched sound. Both sounds have the same loudness.

**Which wave properties are the same for the two sounds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A sound wave's pitch is related to its frequency. The high-pitched sound has a higher frequency than the low-pitched sound.

All sound waves travel at the same speed *in the same medium*. Both speakers are playing in the same air. So, the sounds from both of them travel outward through the air at the same speed.

Since both sound waves travel at the same speed, a higher frequency corresponds to a shorter wavelength, and vice versa. As one increases the other decreases. So, the high-pitched sound has a shorter wavelength than the low-pitched sound.
A sound wave's loudness is related to its amplitude. We're told that both sounds have the same loudness. So, they have the same amplitude.
The wave properties that are the same for the two sounds are speed and amplitude.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two speakers are located in the same room filled with air. Both play sounds with the same pitch. The sound from one speaker is louder than the other.

**Which wave properties are the same for the two sounds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A sound wave's pitch is related to its frequency. Since the two sounds have the same pitch, they have the same frequency.

All sound waves travel at the same speed *in the same medium*. Both speakers are playing in the same air. So, the sounds from both of them travel outward through the air at the same speed.

And since both sound waves travel at the same speed, their equal frequencies mean they must have equal wavelengths too.
A sound wave's loudness is related to its amplitude. We're told the two sounds have different loudnesses. The louder sound has a greater amplitude than the quieter sound.
The wave properties that are the same for the two sounds are speed, frequency, and wavelength.

assessment_items.question_content:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Identify each result described in the table.**

Result | Description
:- | :-
[[☃ dropdown 1]] | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
[[☃ dropdown 2]] | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
[[☃ dropdown 3]] | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary
hints:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, the wave may cross the boundary and travel through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, sound waves are transmitted through air.

It's also possible that the sound wave enters the material, but does not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, sound waves are absorbed by soft materials with many small air pockets, like a pillow or foam.

It's also possible that the sound wave doesn't pass through the boundary. Instead, the wave bounces back and continues traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, sound waves are reflected from hard, dense surfaces like concrete and brick.

The answer is:

Result | Description
:- | :-
transmission | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
absorption | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
reflection | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Complete the descriptions.**

* If the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* If the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 5]].

* If the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material, the wave is [[☃ dropdown 6]].
hints:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, the wave may bounce back and continue traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, sound waves are reflected from hard, dense surfaces like concrete and brick.

It's also possible that the sound wave enters the material, but does not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, sound waves are absorbed by soft materials with many small air pockets, like a pillow or foam.

It's also possible that the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, sound waves are transmitted through air.

The answer is:

* If the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary, the wave is reflected.

* If the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form, the wave is absorbed.

* If the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material, the wave is transmitted.

assessment_items.question_content:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, different results are possible.

**Match each result with its description by dragging the tiles.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
When a sound wave reaches a material boundary, it may enter the material but not continue traveling through it. Instead, the wave's energy is converted to thermal energy or another energy form. This is called **absorption**.

For example, sound waves are absorbed by soft materials with many small air pockets, like a pillow or foam.

It's also possible that the sound wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material. This is called **transmission**. 

For example, sound waves are transmitted through air.

It's also possible that the sound wave doesn't pass through the boundary. Instead, the wave bounces back and continues traveling in its original material. This is called **reflection**.

For example, sound waves are reflected from hard, dense surfaces like concrete and brick.

The answer is:

Result | Description
:- | :-
absorption | the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
transmission | the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
reflection | the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary

right: the wave's energy is taken in by the material and converted to another form
the wave crosses the boundary and travels through the material
the wave bounces back after reaching the boundary

assessment_items.question_content:
A slow-motion model of a periodic sound wave is shown below. Each dot represents an air particle. The gray surface on the left vibrates, causing sound waves to travel through the air.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Analyze the model to complete the descriptions.**

* The sound waves move [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* The individual air particles move [[☃ dropdown 2]].

* The sound waves transfer [[☃ dropdown 3]] from one location to another.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: We can see on the model that the sound waves—the compressions—move from left to right through the air. Each disturbance begins at the vibrating surface on the left, and then travels away from it.

However, the individual air particles move *back and forth* around their equilibrium positions as the sound waves pass by. We can see this by watching a single dot. (A few of the dots are colored $\red{\text{red}}$ for easier tracking.)

So, matter is not transported by the sound waves. Instead, the matter is disturbed and returns to its equilibrium position, while the disturbance itself is transported through the matter. Waves transfer *energy*, not matter.

The answer is:

* The sound waves move from left to right.

* The individual air particles move back and forth.

* The sound waves transfer energy from one location to another.

options.alt: Animation of a sound wave shows many small dots in a tube. A surface at the left end of the tube move back and forth, right and left. This disturbs the dots nearby by pushing them back and forth. The disturbance travels from left to right along the tube as dots farther along the tube also get pushed back and forth.

assessment_items.question_content:
A slow-motion model of a periodic sound wave is shown below. Each dot represents an air particle. The gray surface on the left vibrates, causing sound waves to travel through the air.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Analyze the model and select the true statements.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: We can see on the model that the sound waves—the compressions—move from left to right through the air. Each disturbance begins at the vibrating surface on the left, and then travels away from it.

However, the individual air particles move *back and forth* around their equilibrium positions as the sound waves pass by. We can see this by watching a single dot. (A few of the dots are colored $\red{\text{red}}$ for easier tracking.)

So, matter is not transported by the sound waves. Instead, the matter is disturbed and returns to its equilibrium position, while the disturbance itself is transported through the matter. Waves transfer *energy*, not matter.

The answers are:

* The sound waves move from left to right. The air particles move back and forth.

* The sound waves transfer energy, not matter.

options.alt: Animation of a sound wave shows many small dots in a tube. A surface at the left end of the tube move back and forth, right and left. This disturbs the dots nearby by pushing them back and forth. The disturbance travels from left to right along the tube as dots farther along the tube also get pushed back and forth.

choices.content: The sound waves move back and forth. The air particles move from left to right.
The sound waves move from left to right. The air particles move back and forth.
The sound waves transfer energy, not matter.
The sound waves transfer matter, not energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
A slow-motion model of a periodic sound wave is shown below. Each dot represents an air particle. The gray surface on the left vibrates, causing sound waves to travel through the air.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Analyze the model and complete the predictions.**

* If the gray surface moves back and forth more often, the [[☃ dropdown 1]] of the sound wave will increase.

* If the gray surface moves back and forth a greater distance, the [[☃ dropdown 2]] of the sound wave will increase.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
We can see on the model that the sound waves—the compressions—move from left to right through the air. Each disturbance begins at the vibrating surface on the left, and then travels away from it.

However, the individual air particles move *back and forth* around their equilibrium positions as the sound waves pass by. We can see this by watching a single dot. (A few of the dots are colored $\red{\text{red}}$ for easier tracking.)

The number of wave cycles generated in a certain time amount of time, for example in one second, is the wave's **frequency**.

If the surface moves back and forth more often, the air particles will be pushed back and forth more often. The wave's frequency will increase.

The maximum amount that the air particles are disturbed away from their equilibrium positions is the wave's **amplitude**.

If the surface moves back and forth a greater distance, it will disturb the air particles a greater distance. The wave's amplitude will increase.

How fast individual waves move forward through the air is the wave's **speed**.

Wave speed depends only on the medium. Since the medium—the air—does not change, the wave speed is the same no matter how the gray surface is moving.

The answers are:

* If the gray surface moves back and forth more often, the frequency of the sound wave will increase.

* If the gray surface moves back and forth a greater distance, the amplitude of the sound wave will increase.

options.alt: Animation of a sound wave shows many small dots in a tube. A surface at the left end of the tube move back and forth, right and left. This disturbs the dots nearby by pushing them back and forth. The disturbance travels from left to right along the tube as dots farther along the tube also get pushed back and forth.
